title_en: Sunni Engagement with Al-Kashaf: Appropriation and Critique
title_ar: التعامل السني مع الكشاف: الاستفادة والنقد
content_en:
The history of Sunni scholars' engagement with Al-Kashaf is a study in how the Islamic scholarly tradition manages complex texts — works of genuine value produced from within a heterodox theological framework. The tradition did not simply accept or reject Al-Kashaf; it developed a sophisticated methodology for using it selectively and critically, and this methodology itself became a part of the scholarly heritage.

The earliest major Sunni engagement was that of al-Fakhr al-Razi (544–606 AH / 1149–1210 CE), whose encyclopedic commentary Mafatih al-Ghayb (also known as Tafsir al-Kabir or Tafsir al-Razi) frequently borrows al-Zamakhshari's linguistic insights while arguing against his theological positions. Al-Razi's commentary is arguably the most comprehensive tafsir ever produced, and its engagement with Al-Kashaf — agreeing where al-Zamakhshari's grammar is sound, disagreeing where his theology deviates from Ash'ari Sunni positions — set the template for how later scholars would approach the work.

Al-Baydawi (d. 685 AH / 1286 CE) produced Anwar al-Tanzil wa-Asrar al-Ta'wil, which many scholars consider the most successful 'purified' adaptation of Al-Kashaf. Al-Baydawi took al-Zamakhshari's linguistic analyses, stripped them of their Mu'tazili theological glosses, and replaced the heterodox elements with Ash'ari Sunni positions. The result is a commentary that preserves the linguistic depth of Al-Kashaf while being sound in its theology. Al-Baydawi's commentary became one of the most widely taught tafsirs in the Ottoman scholarly tradition and attracted dozens of supercommentaries (hashiyas) of its own. It is, in a sense, Al-Kashaf made safe for the Sunni madrasa — though advanced students were expected to read the original as well.

Abu Hayyan al-Andalusi (654–745 AH / 1256–1344 CE), the master of Quranic Arabic grammar from Spain, engaged Al-Kashaf extensively in his tafsir Al-Bahr al-Muhit (The Surrounding Ocean). Abu Hayyan frequently disagrees with al-Zamakhshari on grammatical as well as theological grounds — showing that even on the linguistic level, Al-Kashaf's readings are not above scholarly debate. His critique sharpened the tradition's understanding of where al-Zamakhshari's grammatical analysis is sound and where it is driven by his theological commitments.

In the Ottoman scholarly world, Al-Kashaf was a standard advanced text. Students who had mastered Al-Baydawi or Tafsir al-Jalalain were directed to Al-Kashaf for deeper linguistic study, under the guidance of teachers who could navigate its theological complexity. The Ottoman scholarly tradition produced numerous hawashi on Al-Kashaf, including by scholars like Mulla Jami and others, that provided theological commentary on al-Zamakhshari's positions.

This pattern of critical engagement represents the mature Sunni scholarly approach to Al-Kashaf: recognize its linguistic genius, acknowledge its theological deviations from Sunni consensus, study it with the critiques alongside it, and derive from it what is sound while setting aside what is not. This is not compromise — it is the application of the scholars' principle that wisdom is the believer's lost property, to be taken wherever it is found, while false doctrine is identified and refused.
